nativeRange.habitat: Exposed seaward reefs and lagoon reefs, typically found at depths of 0–145 m, commonly singly or in small groups of up to 5 individuals; forms monogamous pairs during breeding

diet.notes: Has been documented feeding on a wide variety of small animal prey including hydroids, fish eggs, small crustaceans, polychaete worms, and the tube feet of echinoderms. In captivity tends to accept frozen mysis shrimp, brine shrimp, finely chopped seafood, and quality frozen carnivore preparations; live blackworms and small crustaceans are often useful for initial coaxing. Frequent small feedings (two to three times daily) are generally advised.

summary: A widespread Indo-Pacific butterflyfish with a distinctive long snout and bright yellow body, generally considered one of the most aquarium-adaptable members of its family. Best kept singly or as a mated pair; reef caution advised.
careNotes: The Yellow Longnose Butterflyfish is generally considered one of the hardiest and most adaptable butterflyfish for marine aquariums, often recommended as a first butterflyfish for keepers ready to commit to a moderately sized fish-only or fish-with-live-rock system. Its long, forceps-like snout is used in the wild to extract small invertebrates from tight crevices and to pick at the tube feet of echinoderms. In captivity it tends to accept frozen and prepared meaty foods reasonably well, though a varied diet and patience during acclimation are generally advised. Reef-safety is best described as 'with caution': specimens have been reported to nip at tubeworms, feather dusters, polyps, and occasionally LPS corals, while many individuals largely ignore SPS and zoanthids. The species is peaceful toward dissimilar tankmates but tends to be aggressive toward conspecifics and other longnose butterflyfish — best kept singly or as a verified mated pair. Forms monogamous pairs in the wild; if a mated pair is acquired, established pairs should not be separated. Active swimmer requiring ample horizontal swimming space alongside rockwork for grazing and shelter; a tank length of at least ~150 cm is generally advised even though the fish itself reaches around 22 cm. Tank-size cross-source disagreement noted: LiveAquaria recommends 125 gal (~473 L), Saltwater Aquarium Blog suggests 75 gal (~284 L) as a starting point; 450 L (~120 US gal) was chosen as a cautious rounded value given the species' adult size and active swimming behavior. Temperature cross-source disagreement noted: LiveAquaria reports 72–78°F (22.2–25.6°C) while FishBase reports 24.3–28.8°C; the averaged range of 23–27°C is used here. Secondary source (Reef2Reef wiki thread) returned HTTP 403; substituted FishBase and Saltwater Aquarium Blog as cross-verification.
breedingNotes: Captive breeding is rarely if ever achieved. In the wild, monogamous pairs are reported to engage in pelagic spawning, with planktonic larvae that drift for an extended period before settlement. Captive reproduction is generally considered impractical under normal aquarium conditions.

fish.breedingNotes: Captive breeding is rarely if ever achieved. Wild pairs are reported to engage in pelagic spawning; larvae are planktonic.
